values: first-time trekkers
trekkers without prior high-altitude experience
trekkers without adequate trek insurance
trekkers with pre-existing medical conditions (must be disclosed)

evidence.quote_or_summary: Altitude sickness can happen to anybody - first-time trekkers as well as experienced trekkers. Fitness helps in making your trek easier. But do not forget that even a fit person climbing too high too soon runs an equal risk of developing AMS.
But, if you are not equipped with an adequate Trek Insurance, this can burn a hole in your pocket.
Please inform your Trek Leader about any pre-existing medical conditions or health concerns, no matter how minor they may seem.

values: Difficult Trek

evidence.quote_or_summary: Difficult Trek
These long distances and high altitudes make Everest Base Camp a difficult trek, suitable only for experienced trekkers.

values: Suitable for Experienced Trekkers

evidence.quote_or_summary: Suitable for Experienced Trekkers
These long distances and high altitudes make Everest Base Camp a difficult trek, suitable only for experienced trekkers.

values: Long distances over 100 km across 12 days
Boulder sections on steep, uneven trails
Boulder fields and glacial moraines (Dingboche to Lobuche)
Glacial moraine walks (Gorakshep to Everest Base Camp)
Uneven terrain punishing on knees (Tengboche descent)
Muddy and slippery trails at lower elevations after rainfall
Slippery trails in rainy or snowy conditions near Namche Bazaar

evidence.quote_or_summary: You trek for more than 100 km over 12 days straight, climbing from 8600 ft to a whooping 18,200 ft at Kalapathar.
Every day will include ascents, descents and boulder sections on steep, uneven trails.
You trek through boulder fields and glacial moraines, experiencing strong winds along the Khumbu Glacier ridge.
The final leg of the trek from Gorakshep (16,942 ft) to Everest Base Camp (17,598 ft) is demanding, with steep ascents and long boulder walks through glacial moraine.
its uneven terrain can be punishing on your knees
Rainfall at the lower elevations like Namche Bazaar and Tengboche, can lead to muddy and slippery trails.
This climb can be challenging, especially in rainy or snowy conditions, making the trail slippery.

values: 600-meter ascent over 2 km just before Namche Bazaar
Steep descent from Namche Bazaar to Phunki Thanga
Sharp climb from Phunki Thanga to Tengboche (12,687 ft)
Steep ascent from Dingboche (14,340 ft) to Lobuche (16,210 ft)
Steep climb to Kala Patthar (18,200 ft) typically done in early morning
Steep ascents and long boulder walks from Gorakshep (16,942 ft) to Everest Base Camp (17,598 ft)
Long trekking hours most days

evidence.quote_or_summary: You need to negotiate a 600-meter ascent over 2 km just before reaching Namche Bazaar.
From Namche Bazaar, the trail drops steeply to Phunki Thanga before climbing sharply to Tengboche (12,687 ft).
The steep ascent from Dingboche (14,340 ft) to Lobuche (16,210 ft) is one of the most challenging sections of the trek.
The trek to Kala Patthar (18,200 ft) is the journey's highest point. This steep climb is typically done in the early morning
The final leg of the trek from Gorakshep (16,942 ft) to Everest Base Camp (17,598 ft) is demanding, with steep ascents and long boulder walks through glacial moraine.
Most days involve trekking for long hours.

values: Micro spikes, gaiters, helmets, ropes provided by Indiahikes where required
Layering required to tackle wind chill at Khumbu Glacier ridge and Kala Patthar

evidence.quote_or_summary: our Trek Leaders and Technical Guides will provide you with all necessary guidance, support, and technical gear such as micro spikes, gaiters, helmets, ropes, etc. wherever required.
The Khumbu Glacier ridge and Kala Patthar are known for strong winds. Layer up well to tackle the wind chill.

values: Acute Mountain Sickness (AMS) risk from Day 2 (Phakding to Namche Bazaar, ~3000 ft gain)
Very high altitudes up to 18,200 ft at Kala Patthar
AMS risk remains even with acclimatization stops at Namche Bazaar and Dingboche
AMS risk above 10,000 ft applies to anyone including first-time and experienced trekkers
Risk of HAPE and HACE referenced

evidence.quote_or_summary: On the second day of your trek, you gain close to 3000 ft in elevation from Phakding to Namche Bazar. This makes you very susceptible to Acute Mountain Sickness.
At the Kala Patthar summit, you stand more than 18,000 ft above sea level. Even with acclimatization stops at Namche Bazaar and Dingboche, Acute Mountain Sickness remains a real risk.
At altitudes above 10,000 ft, the chances of being hit by Acute Mountain Sickness are real. Altitude sickness can happen to anybody - first-time trekkers as well as experienced trekkers.
Here's a Complete guide to Acute Mountains Sickness, HAPE and HACE

values: Spring snowfall likely at higher altitudes (Kala Patthar, Lobuche) making navigation difficult
Rainfall at lower elevations (Namche Bazaar, Tengboche) causing muddy/slippery trails
Possible verglas on Kala Patthar trail in cold early-morning conditions
Strong winds at Khumbu Glacier ridge and Kala Patthar
Trek may be aborted or turned back from any campsite if weather deteriorates

evidence.quote_or_summary: If you are trekking in Spring, snowfall is very likely at the higher altitudes, particularly at Kala Patthar and Lobuche, making navigation difficult.
Rainfall at the lower elevations like Namche Bazaar and Tengboche, can lead to muddy and slippery trails.
cold temperatures and possible verglas on the trail add to the challenge.
The Khumbu Glacier ridge and Kala Patthar are known for strong winds.
If the weather deteriorates, the team may need to turn back from any campsite to ensure the safety of all trekkers.

values: High-altitude medical kits, oxygen cylinders, and stretchers carried by Indiahikes team
Fastest evacuation by helicopter from Gorak Shep or Pheriche (weather/availability dependent)
Nearest hospitals in Lukla and Kathmandu
Basic medical aid at lower-altitude villages like Namche Bazaar
Primary health centres in Namche Bazaar for immediate attention
Trekkers must descend to Lukla quickly in emergencies
Trek insurance strongly advised due to potential helicopter evacuation costs
Pre-existing medical conditions must be disclosed to Trek Leader

evidence.quote_or_summary: The Indiahikes team is equipped with high-altitude medical kits, oxygen cylinders, and stretchers to handle any altitude-related emergencies.
The fastest evacuation route is by helicopter from Gorak Shep or Pheriche, depending on the severity of the emergency.
The nearest hospitals are in Lukla and Kathmandu. Basic medical aid is available at lower-altitude villages like Namche Bazaar.
there are a few primary health centres in Namche Bazaar. In case of an emergency, you must head down to Lukla as quickly as possible.
if you are not equipped with an adequate Trek Insurance, this can burn a hole in your pocket.
Please inform your Trek Leader about any pre-existing medical conditions or health concerns, no matter how minor they may seem.

values: Safety is a shared responsibility; trekkers must actively follow safety protocols
Trekkers must disclose pre-existing medical conditions to Trek Leader
Trek may be turned back from any campsite if weather deteriorates

evidence.quote_or_summary: we believe that safety is a shared responsibility. We need your active participation and attention to safety protocols to ensure a safe and successful trek.
Please inform your Trek Leader about any pre-existing medical conditions or health concerns, no matter how minor they may seem.
If the weather deteriorates, the team may need to turn back from any campsite to ensure the safety of all trekkers.
